1: ἐξουσίαν μεγάλην — great authority: the angel's authority makes him the agent of the verdict pronounced in heaven. ἡ γῆ ἐφωτίσθη ἐκ τῆς δόξης αὐτοῦ — the earth was illumined from his glory: the angel's radiance echoes the vision of the divine glory returning to the temple in Ezekiel 43:2 ("the earth shone with his glory") — a judgment-of-Babylon oracle delivered through a glory-bearing divine agent.
2: ἔπεσεν ἔπεσεν Βαβυλών ἡ μεγάλη — Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the great: the doubled aorist is a direct verbal citation of Isaiah 21:9 LXX (ἔπεσεν ἔπεσεν Βαβυλών); the prophetic past (completed action before the event) signals divine certainty. By quoting Isaiah's Babylon oracle verbatim, Revelation claims that Rome's fall is the fulfillment of Isaiah's word. κατοικητήριον δαιμονίων — habitation of demons: the fallen city becomes the haunt of unclean spirits, fulfilling the pattern of OT judgment-desolation oracles (Isaiah 13:19–22; Revelation 34:11–15; Jeremiah 50:39), where fallen cities become dwelling places for wild creatures and spirits of chaos.
3: θυμοῦ τῆς πορνείας αὐτῆς — the passion/wrath of her sexual immorality: thymos can mean "wrath" (God's in Revelation 14:10, 19; Revelation 15:1, 7; Revelation 16:1, 19) or "fierce passion/desire" (the consuming ardor of idolatrous alliance); both senses may be active — the wine of passionate idolatry that itself produces wrath. ἐπλούτησαν ἐκ τῆς δυνάμεως τοῦ στρήνους αὐτῆς — grew rich from the power of her luxurious excess: strēnos (luxurious excess, sensuality in excess) appears only here and Revelation 18:7 in the NT; the merchants' wealth is not incidental but structurally derived from Rome's culture of excess.
4: ἐξέλθατε ἐξ αὐτῆς ὁ λαός μου — Come out from her, my people (aorist imperative, urgency): the divine call echoes the exodus-from-Babylon cluster of OT oracles — Isaiah 48:20 ("Go out from Babylon!"), Jeremiah 50:8 ("Flee from the midst of Babylon"), Jeremiah 51:6, 45 ("Flee from the midst of Babylon; save your lives"). The Exodus pattern (separation from the empire of oppression) is activated as the model for Christian non-participation in Rome's economic-religious system. ἵνα μή συγκοινωνήσητε ταῖς ἁμαρτίαις αὐτῆς — so that you will not share in her sins: synkoinōneō (to participate with, share in); the practical ethical demand: cultic and economic withdrawal from the imperial system is not optional for those who bear the Lamb's name.
5: ἐκολλήθησαν αὐτῆς αἱ ἁμαρτίαι ἄχρι τοῦ οὐρανοῦ — her sins were joined/piled up to heaven: ekollaō (to glue, join closely together; aorist passive) suggests accretion over time, a pile built course by course until it reaches divine attention; echoes Genesis 18:20 (Sodom's outcry "has reached" YHWH) and Ezra 9:6 ("our iniquities have grown above our heads"). ἐμνημόνευσεν ὁ θεός τά ἀδικήματα αὐτῆς — God has remembered her crimes: the divine memory is judicial, not nostalgic; God's "remembering" in OT covenant theology activates response — saving for Israel (Genesis 8:1; Exodus 2:24) and judging for enemies (cf. Psalms 137:7).
6: ἀπόδοτε αὐτῇ ὡς καί αὐτή ἀπέδωκεν — repay her as she herself repaid (aorist imperatives; divine command to the agents of judgment): lex talionis operating at the imperial scale. διπλώσατε τά διπλᾶ — double the double for her: the double repayment echoes Jeremiah 50:15, 29 ("repay her according to her deeds") and Isaiah 40:2 ("she has received from the LORD's hand double for all her sins"). The cup imagery is sustained: the prostitute poured out her cup of abomination to the nations; now a double measure is poured back.
7: ὅσα ἐδόξασεν ἑαυτήν καί ἐστρηνίασεν — as much as she glorified herself and lived in luxury: the reflexive heautēn (glorified herself) is the root sin; divine glory (Revelation 19:1 — salvation and glory belong to God) has been arrogated to the imperial city. βασίλισσα κάθημαι καί χήρα οὐκ εἰμί — I sit as queen and I am not a widow: the boast directly echoes Isaiah 47:7–8 (the fall of Babylon: "I shall be queen forever... I am, and there is no one besides me; I shall not sit as a widow"); Revelation applies Isaiah's Babylon oracle to Rome verse by verse.
8: ἐν μιᾷ ἡμέρᾳ — in one day: the suddenness of divine judgment against the empire's self-confident security; centuries of accumulated power collapse in a single day. θάνατος καί πένθος καί λιμός — death and mourning and famine: precisely the three divine judgments of Ezekiel 5:12; Revelation 14:21 (sword, famine, pestilence), here deployed against the city that boasted "I will not see grief" (Revelation 18:7). κύριος ὁ θεός ὁ κρίνας αὐτήν — Lord God who has judged her: the aorist krinas (having judged) grounds the entire judgment sequence in the divine character and judicial act; Babylon's destruction is not political accident but covenantal verdict.
9: κλαύσουσιν καί κόψονται ἐπ' αὐτήν — will weep and beat their breasts over her: koptomai (to beat oneself in grief) was the conventional mourning posture of the ancient world. οἱ μετ' αὐτῆς πορνεύσαντες καί στρηνιάσαντες — those who committed fornication with her and lived in luxury with her: the mourners are Rome's political clients who profited from the system; their grief is economic loss, not moral repentance — the same self-interest that joined them to Babylon now laments its passing.
10: ἀπό μακρόθεν ἑστηκότες — standing far off (present participle, sustained safe distance): the kings do not approach the burning city; their grief is calculated self-preservation disguised as mourning. ἐν μιᾷ ὥρᾳ ἦλθεν ἡ κρίσις σου — in one hour your judgment has come: the first of the three "one-hour" refrains (Revelation 18:10, 17, 19) that punctuate the three laments (kings, merchants, sailors); the contrast between Rome's centuries-long dominion and the single hour of her collapse is the central irony of the chapter.
11: οἱ ἔμποροι τῆς γῆς κλαίουσιν καί πενθοῦσιν ἐπ' αὐτήν — the merchants of the earth weep and mourn over her (present tense — visionary immediacy): emporos (merchant, long-distance trader); the mourning is commercial — ὅτι τόν γόμον αὐτῶν οὐδεῖς ἀγοράζει οὐκέτι (because no one buys their cargo anymore). The merchants' grief is indistinguishable from the kings': both mourn the loss of a profitable patron, not the moral collapse of a corrupt system.
12: The catalog of luxury goods begins with the most precious (gold, silver, precious stones, pearls) and moves through textiles (fine linen, purple, silk, scarlet), then specialty woods and goods. ξύλον θύινον — citron/thyine wood: from the sandarac tree (Callitris articulata) of North Africa, prized for its fragrant, beautifully grained wood used in expensive furniture; Pliny the Elder (NH 13.29) describes the Roman obsession with citron-wood tables as a luxury mania. ἐλέφαντα — ivory: imported primarily from sub-Saharan Africa and India. The catalog maps the reach of Rome's trade network across the known world.
13: The catalog continues through aromatics (cinnamon, spice, incense, myrrh, frankincense — all imported from Arabia and India via the Red Sea trade), foodstuffs (wine, olive oil, fine flour, wheat), and livestock (cattle, sheep, horses, chariots). σώματα καί ψυχάς ἀνθρώπων — bodies and souls of human beings: the list climaxes with enslaved people. Sōmata (bodies) was standard commercial Greek for slaves; the addition of psychas anthrōpōn (souls of humans) is John's editorial exposure of the dehumanization: Rome's economy treats human souls as cargo. The slave trade was foundational to the Roman economy — estimates suggest 30% of Italy's population were enslaved in the first century.
14: ἡ ὀπώρα σου τῆς ἐπιθυμίας τῆς ψυχῆς — the ripe fruit of your soul's desire: opōra (late-summer ripe fruit, the harvest season's abundance); the word carries connotations of seasonal fullness and luxury now permanently ended (ἀπώλετο ἀπό σοῦ — perished from you). The merchants' lament personalizes the loss: they had identified their soul's longing with Babylon's luxury; now their psyche (soul) is exposed as having invested in what perishes.
15: ἀπό μακρόθεν στήσονται — they will stand far off: the merchant lament repeats the spatial image of the king lament (Revelation 18:10); commercial self-interest produces the same calculated distance as political self-preservation. The parallel structure of the three laments (kings Revelation 18:9–10; merchants Revelation 18:11–17; sailors Revelation 18:17–19) mirrors the prophetic lament structure of Ezek 27 over Tyre, which Revelation deliberately echoes throughout chapter 18.
16: οὐαί οὐαί ἡ πόλις ἡ μεγάλη — Woe! Woe to the great city: the doubled ouai (woe) repeats the prophetic woe-oracle formula (Isaiah 5:8–23; Amos 5:18; Revelation 6:1); the merchants who dressed the city in her luxury now lament her stripping. ἡ περιβεβλημένη βύσσινον καί πορφυροῦν καί κόκκινον — clothed in fine linen and purple and scarlet: the specific textiles of 17:4 reappear in the lament, confirming the merchants' complicity in the prostitute's display; they supplied her dress and now mourn its destruction.
17: ἐν μιᾷ ὥρᾳ ἠρημώθη ὁ τοσοῦτος πλοῦτος — in one hour such great wealth was laid waste: second "one hour" refrain. Ērēmōthē (was laid waste, desolated; aorist passive) uses the same root as the wilderness (erēmos) of Revelation 17:3, where John first saw the prostitute seated in desolation; the fallen city returns to the wasteland state. κυβερνήτης καί πᾶς ὁ ἐπί τόπον πλέων — every helmsman and all who sail to any place: the shipping industry that profited from Mediterranean commerce.
18: τίς ὁμοία τῇ πόλει τῇ μεγάλῃ — What city was like the great city? The rhetorical question is the sailors' lament echoing Ezekiel 27:32 ("Who is like Tyre, like one destroyed in the midst of the sea?"); Tyre the great trading city of the ancient world serves as the prophetic template for Rome. The rhetorical form implies that no rival exists — and now the unrivaled city is gone. The question is unanswerable because the answer was always meant to be "none," and now the greatness that prompted the question has vanished.
19: ἔβαλον χοῦν ἐπί τάς κεφαλάς αὐτῶν — they threw dust on their heads: the ancient mourning rite (Joshua 7:6; Job 2:12; Lamentations 2:10; Ezekiel 27:30 — explicitly in the Tyre lament); the sailors add dust to the weeping and wailing, completing the mourning-rite vocabulary. ἐκ τῆς τιμιότητος αὐτῆς — from her costliness: timiotēs (preciousness, high commercial value) appears only here in the NT; the word captures Rome's function as the center of Mediterranean commerce — her "value" was entirely economic. The third "one hour" refrain closes the triad.
20: εὐφραίνου ἐπ' αὐτῇ οὐρανέ — Rejoice over her, O heaven: the heaven/earth reversal crystallizes the chapter's structure: while earth's three classes of profiteers mourn (Revelation 18:9–19), heaven is commanded to rejoice. The prophetic call echoes Isaiah 44:23; Revelation 49:13 (calls for cosmic rejoicing at divine deliverance). ἔκρινεν ὁ θεός τό κρίμα ὑμῶν ἐξ αὐτῆς — God has judged your judgment from her: the reflexive justice — the krima (verdict) that Babylon executed against the saints is the exact verdict executed against Babylon; the lex talionis operating at cosmic scale through the divine judge.
21: λίθον ὡς μύλινον μέγαν — a stone like a great millstone: the prophetic action-sign echoes Jeremiah 51:63–64, where Jeremiah tied a stone to a scroll of Babylon's judgment and threw it into the Euphrates as a sign of the city's sinking to rise no more. Revelation intensifies: an angel of great authority throws the stone. ὁρμήματι βληθήσεται Βαβυλών ἡ μεγάλη πόλις — with violent force Babylon the great city will be thrown: hormēma (violent rushing force, momentum); the finality and speed of the destruction matches the "one hour" imagery of the laments.
22: The sevenfold "never again" announcement (ou mē + subjunctive — the strongest Greek negation) structures Revelation 18:22–23: harpists, musicians, flutists, trumpeters; craftsmen of every craft; the sound of the millstone; the light of the lamp; the voice of bride and bridegroom. The silencing of music and craft echoes Jeremiah 7:34; Revelation 25:10 (YHWH's judgment on Jerusalem removes the sounds of joy and human productivity); now applied to Rome. φωνή νυμφίου καί νύμφης — voice of bridegroom and bride: the joyful domestic life of the city permanently extinguished; the anti-type to the marriage supper of the Lamb (Revelation 19:7–9) that replaces it.
23: φῶς λύχνου οὐ μή φάνῃ ἐν σοί ἔτι — the light of a lamp will never shine in you again: the domestic lamp, the most basic sign of inhabited life, is extinguished permanently. ὅτι οἱ ἔμποροί σου ἦσαν οἱ μεγιστᾶνες τῆς γῆς — because your merchants were the great ones of the earth: Rome elevated commercial power to political status; the economic lords were the political lords. ἐν τῇ φαρμακείᾳ σου ἐπλανήθησαν πάντα τά ἔθνη — by your sorcery all the nations were deceived: pharmakeia (the use of drugs, potions, or occult arts; whence "pharmacy") — in apocalyptic context, Rome's seductive imperial cult with its rituals, spectacle, and economic coercion is named as sorcery. Epananēthēsan (were deceived/led astray): the same verb used for Satan's activity (Revelation 12:9; Revelation 20:3, 8).
24: ἐν αὐτῇ αἷμα προφητῶν καί ἁγίων εὑρέθη — in her was found the blood of prophets and holy ones: heurethē (was found, divine passive) — the forensic finding establishes legal guilt; divine search has located the evidence. πάντων τῶν ἐσφαγμένων ἐπί τῆς γῆς — of all who have been slaughtered on the earth: esphagmenōn (from sphazō = to slaughter, the same verb used for the Lamb at Revelation 5:6, 9, 12; Revelation 13:8) — the martyred saints share the vocabulary of the Lamb's slaughter. Rome's guilt extends beyond Christian martyrs to all the slaughtered throughout history; the empire of blood is held accountable for blood.
